draft.introduction: Hebrews 2:1–18 argues that Christ was made lower than the angels for a time so that through death he might destroy the power of death. The passage grounds soteriology in the full reality of the incarnation. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: But we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death, so that by the grace of God he might taste death for everyone. For it was fitting that he, for whom and by whom all things exist, in bringing many sons to glory, should make the founder of their salvation perfect through suffering.
translation.original_text: τον δε βραχυ τι παρ αγγελους ηλαττωμενον βλεπομεν ιησουν

translation.text: Since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil. For surely it is not angels that he helps, but he helps the offspring of Abraham. Therefore he had to be made like his brothers in every respect.
translation.original_text: επει ουν τα παιδια κεκοινωνηκεν αιματος και σαρκος

translator_notes.note: The claim that God made the “founder of salvation perfect through suffering” (δια παθηματων τελειωσαι) implies that Christ’s human experience was not incidental but necessary. The verb teleioo means to bring to completion or maturity, not to correct a deficiency.
